turns.content: Hey, I'm trying to expose a Rust function so it's visible in C code. Should I use #[no_mangle] or #[export_name]? What’s the difference?

Good question! Both `#[no_mangle]` and `#[export_name = "custom"]` control how Rust exports symbols, but they do slightly different things. Using `#[no_mangle]` prevents Rust's default name mangling, making the symbol visible in C without renaming it. Meanwhile, `#[export_name = "custom"]` explicitly renames the function to whatever you specify, which can be useful for clarity or avoiding collisions.
Okay, so if I use #[no_mangle], does that mean the symbol name will exactly match my Rust function name?
Yes, `#[no_mangle]` keeps the exact name you give in your code. But be careful: if two symbols with the same name exist, it's undefined behavior (UB) when linking, so always check for collisions.
Hmm, I see. Should I combine `#[no_mangle]` and `pub extern "C" fn`?

Absolutely! Using `pub extern "C" fn` declares a public function with C calling conventions, while adding `#[no_mangle]` ensures it won't be mangled. This is the common pattern for FFI (Foreign Function Interface) — making Rust functions callable from C code.
Got it. And what if I want to rename my symbol explicitly?
`#[export_name = "custom"]` is perfect for that — you get total control over the name, which can help avoid conflicts or match C naming conventions better.
What about safety implications? When should I be careful?

Always check for symbol collisions when using either attribute. If two functions have the same name, it leads to undefined behavior and hard-to-debug crashes. Also, remember that unsafe code is involved here — calling foreign C libraries or linking with embedded scripts requires extra caution.
Great, thanks! So I just need to be mindful of naming conflicts and use `#[no_mangle]` for exact names, or `#[export_name]` when renaming?
Exactly! And always declare C calling conventions with `extern "C" fn`, then decorate as needed. That way you avoid name mangling pitfalls and ensure safe FFI interactions.

I'm working through a tutorial on Rust attributes, specifically #[no_mangle] and #[export_name]. I've got this working code snippet: `#[no_mangle]
pub extern "C" fn my_function() { /* ... */ }`. Is there a better way to do it?

Good question! Your snippet is correct. But sometimes, you want more control over the exported symbol name, like renaming it from `my_function` in Rust to something else for C compatibility. In that case, you can use `#[export_name = "custom"]` instead of #[no_mangle]. This allows you to explicitly set a custom name.
Oh right, so does `#[export_name]` override `#[no_mangle]`?
Correct! `#[export_name = "custom"]` is a shorthand that combines both `[no_mangle]` and the renaming effect. It's more concise if you want to rename your exported symbol.
Makes sense. So what are the safety implications of using these attributes?

Good point! Symbol collisions can lead to undefined behavior (UB). If you have two symbols with the same name in your binary, it's not clear which one will be called at runtime. That's why you should choose unique names for exported symbols, especially when linking with C code or embedded systems.
What if I forget to use both? Do I risk UB?

If you just use `#[no_mangle]` without renaming, the symbol will be exported with its Rust mangled name. That's fine for internal FFI calls but won't work well if you're trying to link with C code that expects a specific name. So always consider renaming explicitly unless you need the default mangling.
Alright, so when should I use each one?

Use `#[no_mangle]` if you want to export a symbol with its Rust name but don't need renaming. Use `#[export_name = "custom"]` when you specifically want to rename the exported symbol for C compatibility or avoid collisions.
